risk_name: Long-Lead Electrical Equipment Procurement Delay (Transformers, Switchgear)

description.summary: Large power transformers (110-380 kV, 50-200 MVA) and HV switchgear for electrolyzer grid connections have 18-36 month lead times globally. Competing demand from renewable energy projects, data centers, and grid infrastructure creates a procurement bottleneck that can delay project commissioning.
description.detailed: A 100-200 MW electrolyzer requires a dedicated high-voltage transformer (typically 50-200 MVA) and associated switchgear. Global transformer manufacturing capacity is stretched: lead times have extended from 12-18 months (pre-2020) to 18-36 months (2024-2026) due to: renewable energy buildout, data center construction, grid modernization, and supply chain disruptions for electrical steel and copper. This is NOT a hydrogen-specific risk — it affects all large industrial electrical connections. However, hydrogen projects compete with these other sectors for the same manufacturing slots. The lead time for a large transformer now exceeds the construction duration of some hydrogen plants.
description.root_cause: Global transformer manufacturing capacity shortage. Competing demand from renewable energy, data centers, electrification, and grid infrastructure. Concentrated supply chain (few manufacturers for large HV transformers).
trigger_events: Transformer OEM quotes lead time >24 months at time of order
Electrical steel (GOES) price increases >20% within 6 months
Transformer factory acceptance test fails, requiring rework and delaying delivery by 3-6 months

assessment.probability_rationale: Transformer lead times of 18-36 months are the industry norm as of 2024-2026. This is not a risk — it's a known constraint that must be managed through early procurement. >40% of European renewable projects report electrical equipment procurement delays.
assessment.impact_rationale: A 6-month transformer delay at 100 MW: €10-15M lost revenue. However, this risk is manageable through early ordering (before FID if necessary). The impact is a function of procurement timing, not an uncontrollable external event.
assessment.detectability_rationale: Transformer lead times are quoted at order and tracked through manufacturing milestones. Factory acceptance testing provides a clear milestone. 6-12 month detection window if procurement is timely.
assessment.assessment_method: Industry benchmark (transformer lead time surveys) + Gold Dataset evidence

preventive_actions.description: Order long-lead electrical equipment (transformer, HV switchgear) at FID, not at construction start. For projects with 18-24 month construction schedule, this means ordering 24-30 months before target COD.
Include transformer delivery buffer of minimum 6 months in project master schedule

incident_descriptions: Holland Hydrogen I (GA-PR-003): Required new Amaliahaven 380 kV substation from TenneT. Mammoet transported 165-tonne transformers. Shell's early engagement with TenneT and direct transformer procurement strategy mitigated this risk.

Hamburg Green Hydrogen Hub (GA-PR-004): BENEFITED from existing 380 kV connection at Moorburg coal plant — no new transformer required. This is a major brownfield advantage explicitly cited in project documentation.

sources.title: IEA Electricity Grids and Secure Energy Transitions 2025 — Transformer supply chain
sources.author: IEA
